segments.paradiso-22-g81: La carne = 'the flesh/body.' d’i is the contracted form of di + i (of the), so d’i mortali = 'of the mortals' (i.e. of human beings). È is the present indicative 'is.' Tanto blanda = 'so/so very weak/pliant' (tanto intensifies the adjective blanda, here meaning feeble rather than soothing).
segments.paradiso-22-g82: Che introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). Giù = 'down/here below' (adverbial), placed before the verb for emphasis. Non basta is the present tense 3rd-person singular 'is not enough/suffice' with subject buon cominciamento ('a good beginning'). Dal ... al ... is a from‑... to‑... phrase: dal = da + il 'from the,' and uses infinitives (nascer, far) in a nominal sense ('the being born' / 'the making'); de la is the archaic/poetic form of di + la ('of the').
segments.paradiso-22-g83: Pier cominciò = 'Peter began' (cominciò is past historic/past tense in narrative). Sanz’ is a contracted/syncope form of senza and means 'without'; the apostrophe marks the elision. Oro and argento are literal 'gold' and 'silver' but stand for wealth/precious metal in a moral/figurative sense; the repeated sanz’ ... e sanz’ ... emphasizes both absences.
segments.paradiso-22-g84: E io = 'and I.' Con + noun (con orazione, con digiuno) marks means/manner: 'by/with prayer' and 'by/with fasting.' The construction e ... e ... links Dante's practice in parallel to Peter's. Orazione here is 'prayer' (devotional practice), digiuno is 'fasting.'
segments.paradiso-22-g85: E Francesco = 'and Francis.' Umilmente is an adverb 'humbly' modifying the implied verb (he humbled himself or acted humbly); il suo convento = 'his convent' (suo agrees with convento). The line compresses action (humbling/giving up possessions) into a short descriptive clause.
segments.paradiso-22-g86: E se guardi = 'and if you look' (guardi is 2nd-person singular present subjunctive/imperative-like or present indicative used here as a polite/poetic imperative: 'if you look/consider'). ’l principio = contracted il principio 'the beginning' (principio = origin). Di ciascuno = 'of each one' (ciascuno = each person/each case). The clause invites the addressee to inspect origins.
segments.paradiso-22-g87: Poscia = 'afterwards/then.' Riguardi here = subjunctive or imperative/poetic present 'you consider/you look back' (riguardare used intransitively as 'consider/regard'). Là dov’ è trascorso = 'there where it/that has passed' (dov’ = dove, è trascorso = perfect/periphrastic 'has passed' or 'has ended up'), so the clause tells the reader to then trace what became of those beginnings.
segments.paradiso-22-g88: Tu vederai = 'you will see' (future tense, second person singular). Del bianco fatto bruno = 'of the white made brown' or more idiomatically 'white turned to brown' — del = di + il 'of the' or 'some of the'; bianco = whiteness/what is white (symbolic purity), fatto is the past participle 'made/turned,' and bruno = 'brown/dark' (indicating moral or physical darkening). The phrase states the observable result of those origins.
segments.paradiso-22-g89: Veramente = adverb ‘truly/indeed’ modifying the whole clause. Iordan is the subject (the river Jordan). vòlto retrorso is a participial phrase (vòlto = past participle of volgere, retrorso = ‘turned back’) functioning as predicate complement; fu is the passato remoto (3rd singular) of essere, so the literal order is emphatically inverted for poetic effect (literally ‘Truly Jordan turned-back was’ = ‘Truly the Jordan was turned back’).
segments.paradiso-22-g90: e = conjunction ‘and’. ’l = elided article (il) before a vowel-marking apostrophe. mar = 'mare' (the sea) is the noun subject of the short clause. fuggir is the infinitive form of fuggire but here functions elliptically in parallel with the previous predicate to mean ‘fled/ran away’ (a poetic usage where the verb form stands in for a past action).
segments.paradiso-22-g91: quando = temporal conjunction ‘when’. Dio is the subject. volse is passato remoto (3rd sing.) of volgere/volcere ‘to turn’ (here ‘when God turned’); the verb is concise and elliptical — it implies God turning his will/face, causing the miracle described in the previous clauses.
segments.paradiso-22-g92: mirabile a veder is a fixed exclamatory phrase ‘marvelous to behold’ (mirabile = adjective, a + infinitive veder = ‘to see’). che introduces the clause that follows ('that here the aid'), qui = ‘here’, ’l = il (the), soccorso = noun ‘aid/rescue’; the clause is somewhat elliptical (no copula), common in poetic Italian: literally ‘marvelous to see that here the aid (is)’.
segments.paradiso-22-g93: Così = 'thus/so' introducing reported speech. mi is the indirect object clitic ‘to me’. disse is passato remoto (3rd singular) of dire (‘he said’). Word order places the adverb first for emphasis: 'Thus to me he said.'
segments.paradiso-22-g94: e = 'and'. indi here means ‘then/from there’ (a temporal adverb) — note: the provided senses list did not include this meaning, so its index is -1. si raccolse is a reflexive past action (si = reflexive clitic; raccolse = passato remoto of raccogliersi) meaning ‘he gathered himself’ or ‘he withdrew/gathered together’; al = a + il ‘to the’; suo collegio = ‘his company/circle’ (collegio here = group/assembly).
segments.paradiso-22-g95: e = 'and'. ’l collegio = 'the company' (il collegio). si strinse: si = reflexive clitic; strinse = passato remoto (3rd sing.) of stringere meaning here ‘drew together’ or ‘closed ranks’; the reflexive construction indicates the company contracted itself.
segments.paradiso-22-g96: poi = adverb ‘then/afterwards’, introducing the next action.
segments.paradiso-22-g97: come = comparative 'like/as'. turbo = noun 'whirlwind/storm' used in a simile to describe the motion that follows.
segments.paradiso-22-g98: in sù = prepositional phrase ‘upward/into the sky’ (in + sù). tutto = adverbial use here ‘completely/entirely’ modifying the verb. s’ is the clitic contraction of si (reflexive), attached to avvolse; avvolse is passato remoto (3rd sing.) of avvolgere meaning ‘wrapped/enveloped’ — s’avvolse therefore ‘it wrapped itself up’ or ‘it rolled itself upward’ (reflexive perfective action).
segments.paradiso-22-g20: Ma is the simple adversative conjunction 'but.' Rivolgiti is a 2nd-person singular imperative with enclitic reflexive -ti (rivolgere → rivolgerti) meaning 'turn (yourself)/direct your attention.' Omai is an adverb 'now/by now,' placed before the directional phrase for emphasis. Inverso altrui uses inverso as a prepositional/adverbial idea 'toward' (literally 'in the direction of') and altrui is an old-form adjective/pronoun meaning 'others' (i.e. other people).
segments.paradiso-22-g21: Ch’ is the apocopated form of che introducing a clause of explanation ('for' / 'that'). Assai here functions adverbially to mean 'much' or 'many' (i.e. a large number). Illustri qualifies spiriti: an attributive adjective 'illustrious.' Vedrai is the future indicative 2nd-person singular 'you will see,' straight statement of expectation.
segments.paradiso-22-g22: Se introduces a conditional clause ('if'). Com’ is the elided form of come 'as' (the apostrophe marks the dropped vowel). Io is the subject pronoun 'I' used for emphasis. Dico is the present indicative 'I say.' L’ is the elided definite article before a vowel (the = 'the'). Aspetto here means 'appearance' or 'look' (what is shown/visible). Redui is the 1st-person singular present (or presentive) of reduo in the sense 'bring back/restore' — here 'I restore/bring back' (poetic/older verb form).
segments.paradiso-22-g23: Come a lei piacque is an impersonal/agentive construction equivalent to 'as it pleased her' or 'as she wished.' Come = 'as'; a lei is the indirect object 'to her'; piacque is passato remoto/preterite 'it pleased' (i.e. 'she desired/allowed it').
segments.paradiso-22-g24: Li occhi ritornai is an unusual word order (object before verb) meaning 'I turned my eyes back' or 'I returned my gaze.' Li occhi is the direct object 'the eyes' (here 'my eyes' understood from context) and ritornai is the first-person singular past tense 'I turned back/returned.'
segments.paradiso-22-g25: E vidi is straightforward past-tense narration 'and I saw.' Cento sperule 'a hundred little pearls' — sperule is a diminutive plural. Che insieme più s’abbellivan is a relative clause: che = 'which,' insieme 'together,' più here 'more' or 'even more,' s’abbellivan is imperfect reflexive 'they adorned themselves' (si + abbellivano) meaning they were made more beautiful. Con mutüi rai 'with mutual rays' — mutüi is an adjective 'mutual' modifying rai 'rays' (poetic image of light spokes).
segments.paradiso-22-g26: Io stava is the imperfect 'I was/stood' indicating a continuing state. Come quei che introduces a simile 'like one who...' Che is the relative 'who.' ’N sé repreme uses the enclitic form of 'in sé' (in himself) and repreme is present-tense (or habitual) 'represses/presses down' — the image is restraining the sharp point (la punta) of desire (del disio). Word order places the simile and relative clause after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.paradiso-22-g27: E non s’attenta di domandar: e non = 'and not'; s’attenta is a reflexive verbal idiom here meaning 'dares' or 'attempts' (si attenta di = 'attempts/dares to'); with non it becomes 'does not dare to ask.' Di introduces the infinitive domandar 'to ask.'
segments.paradiso-22-g28: Sì del troppo si teme is a short clause 'so (much) because of too much he fears (itself)' — sì = 'so' or 'thus'; del troppo = 'of the excess' / 'of too much' (i.e. fearing to ask because of excessiveness); si teme is a reflexive/impersonal use 'one fears' or 'he fears.'
segments.paradiso-22-g29: E la maggiore e la più luculenta di quelle margherite innanzi fessi is somewhat compressed: e la maggiore e la più luculenta = 'and the larger and most splendid'; di quelle margherite = 'of those little marginal flowers/daisies' (margherite is a diminutive/poetic image); innanzi fessi is a poetic locution meaning 'I placed before (me)' or 'I set forward' — fessi here is a poetically inflected past form meaning 'I made/placed' (older/poetic usage). The adjectives precede the noun for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-22-g30: Per far di sé la mia voglia contenta: per + infinitive 'far' gives purpose 'in order to make.' Di sé means 'of itself' or 'for itself' (i.e. by its own means), la mia voglia contenta means 'my desire content/pleased' — contenta is past participle/adjective 'satisfied.' The whole phrase means 'in order to satisfy my desire.'
segments.paradiso-22-g31: Poi = 'then' advancing the narrative. Dentro a lei 'within her' places the hearing as occurring inside the speaker (literally 'into her' meaning inside that person). Udi’ is the past tense 'I heard' (udi’ is elided form of udii or udì), first person singular.
segments.paradiso-22-g32: «Se tu vedessi» is an imperfect-subjunctive conditional 'If you were to see' (polite / hypothetical). Com’ io la carità che tra noi arde: com' = 'how' (introducing manner), io is emphatic 'I' (here functioning syntactically with dico omitted later), la carità is the theological 'charity' (divine love), che tra noi arde is a relative clause 'that burns among/ between us' — arde is present indicative 'burns' used literally/metaphorically of burning love.
segments.paradiso-22-g33: Li tuoi concetti are the direct object 'your thoughts/ideas' (li = plural article/marker with tuoi concetti). Sarebbero is the conditional of essere 'they would be,' forming 'would be expressed.' Espressi is the past participle 'expressed' used predicatively to complete the conditional construction.
segments.paradiso-22-g1: "Oppresso" is a past participle used adjectivally (masculine singular) meaning 'overwhelmed'; the prepositional phrase "di stupore" (di + noun) indicates the cause ('by/with astonishment'). The whole short phrase is a fronted absolute-style participial phrase describing the speaker's state.
segments.paradiso-22-g2: The phrase puts the indirect object and prepositional phrase before the verb: "a la mia guida" = 'to my guide'. "Mi" is the first-person clitic (reflexive/indirect object) attached to the verb. "Volsi" is the first-person singular passato remoto of volgersi/volgere ('I turned [myself]').
segments.paradiso-22-g3: "Come" introduces a simile ('like/as'). "Parvol" (archaic for parvulo) is a diminutive 'little child'. The relative clause "che ricorre sempre colà dove più si confida" uses present indicative "ricorre" ('resorts/returns') and an impersonal/reflexive "si confida" (si = impersonal/oneself) meaning 'where one trusts/feels confident most.' Word order is typical poetic placement (adverbs and relative clauses following the head).
segments.paradiso-22-g4: A simple connective and demonstrative: "e" = 'and', "quella" = 'that one/she' (demonstrative pronoun referring back to the guide). The comma indicates a brief pause before the ensuing simile.
segments.paradiso-22-g5: "Come madre" begins a second simile ('like a mother'). "Che soccorre sùbito al figlio" is a relative clause with present indicative "soccorre" ('helps/comes to aid') and the adverb "sùbito" ('immediately'). "Al figlio palido e anelo" attaches two descriptive words to the child; Dante uses the noun "anelo" ('yearning') attributively to convey the child's anxious/breathless state. "Con la sua voce" specifies the instrument/means (with her voice) which links forward to the verb "disse" that follows.
segments.paradiso-22-g6: "Che" is a relative pronoun referring back to "voce" ('which'). "’L suol" is an archaic contraction of "(essa) suole" (is wont); here "suol ben disporre" = 'is wont to set/put right well' where "disporre" is the infinitive meaning 'arrange/adjust'. This relative clause qualifies the voice just mentioned.
segments.paradiso-22-g7: "Mi" is the indirect object clitic 'to me'; "disse" is the passato remoto of dire ('(she) said'). The colon after the verb introduces direct speech.
segments.paradiso-22-g8: Direct question: "Non sai tu" uses negation + present indicative "sai" ('do you not know'), with the pronoun "tu" placed after the verb (inverted for emphasis). The subordinate clause "che tu se’ in cielo" uses an archaic contraction "se’" (= sei, 'you are'). "In cielo" is the locative prepositional phrase 'in heaven'.
segments.paradiso-22-g9: A continuation of the question: "e non sai tu" repeats the interrogative pattern. "Che 'l cielo è tutto santo" is a declarative subordinate clause: "è" is present indicative of essere ('is'), "tutto" here means 'whole/entire' modifying "santo" ('holy'). Word order places the clause after the question phrase.
segments.paradiso-22-g10: "E ciò che ci si fa" = 'and that which is done there': "ci" is locative ('there') and "si" creates an impersonal/passive construction (one/it is done). "Vien da buon zelo" uses present "vien" (comes) + prepositional phrase "da buon zelo" meaning 'from/by good zeal' (zelo = zeal/diligence); the whole is a rhetorical question confirming motive/source.
segments.paradiso-22-g11: "Come t’avrebbe trasmutato il canto" uses the conditional perfect construction (avrebbe + past participle) to express an unreal/potential result: 'how the song would have transformed you.' "T'" is the clitic object 'you'. Poetic word order places the object after the introductory "come".
segments.paradiso-22-g12: "E io ridendo" is a parenthetical phrase: "io" (I) plus the gerund "ridendo" ('laughing'), indicating the speaker's manner while the hypothetical transformation is described. The gerund qualifies the action and is not the main verb of the sentence.
segments.paradiso-22-g13: "Mo" is an archaic adverb meaning 'now' or 'at this moment'. The inverted order "pensar lo puoi" places the infinitive before the modal verb (poetic inversion) and means 'you can now imagine it' ("lo" = 'it').
segments.paradiso-22-g14: "Poscia che" = 'after/since' introduces a temporal clause. "’L grido t’ha mosso cotanto" uses passato prossimo (ha + past participle "mosso") meaning 'the cry has moved/stirred you so much'; "t'" is the clitic object 'you', and "cotanto" is an adverb 'to such an extent.'
segments.paradiso-22-g15: "Nel qual" ('in which') introduces a relative clause referring back to the preceding event/situation (the cry and its effect). It functions as the connector to the conditional-hypothetical sentence that follows.
segments.paradiso-22-g16: This is a counterfactual conditional in the past: "se 'nteso avessi i prieghi suoi" = 'if you had understood his entreaties.' Grammatically, Dante uses the pluperfect/subjunctive-like construction (past participle + imperfect subjunctive of avere). Note: "'nteso" is an archaic form of "inteso" (understood).
segments.paradiso-22-g17: "Già ti sarebbe nota la vendetta" uses the conditional "sarebbe" + adjective "nota" meaning 'the vengeance would already have been known to you' ("già" = already). The second clause "che tu vedrai innanzi che tu muoi" is a relative/explanatory clause with a future "vedrai" ('you will see') and temporal "innanzi che... muoi" ('before you die').
segments.paradiso-22-g18: "La spada di qua sù" is a locative expression 'the sword here above' ("di qua sù" = 'from here up / up here'). The negative construction "non... né" connects two adverbial/qualitative terms: "non taglia in fretta né tardo" means 'it does not cut hurriedly nor slowly' ("taglia" present indicative 'cuts', and "in fretta" / "tardo" express manner).
segments.paradiso-22-g19: "Ma' ch'al parer di colui" = 'but rather according to the perception/opinion of him': "ma'" is an elided form of "ma" ('but'), "ch'" = "che" ('that/which'), and "al parer di colui" literally 'to the appearance/opinion of that man' (i.e. 'as it appears to him'). The final clause "che disïando o temendo l'aspetta" is a relative clause describing that man: "disïando o temendo" are gerunds ('desiring or fearing') and "l'aspetta" ('awaits it') is present indicative with the clitic "l'" = 'it' (the sword). Note: "parere" is used as a noun here (opinion/appearance).
segments.paradiso-22-g140: Quindi (here) is temporal/sequence 'then' or 'thereupon'. m’apparve is the contracted form of mi apparve, simple past (passato remoto) 'appeared to me'; the clitic mi precedes the verb and is elided as m’. il temperar is a substantive infinitive (the action/name of tempering/mixing) with the definite article; di Giove marks possession/agent 'of Jupiter'. tra 'between' governs the two coordinated noun phrases l’ padre e l’ figlio (elided article l’ before padre/figlio), indicating location/relationship.
segments.paradiso-22-g141: e quindi mi fu chiaro: e 'and', quindi again 'then/thereupon'; mi fu chiaro is an impersonal or experiential construction with essere in passato remoto (fu) + adjective chiaro 'it was clear to me' (mi = to me). il varïar is an infinitive used as a noun 'the varying/change'; che fanno is a relative clause 'that they make' with fanno (present indicative) referring to the celestial motions; di lor dove is a frozen locution meaning 'of their places/whereabouts' (di + lor 'their'), where dove functions as 'place'.
segments.paradiso-22-g142: e tutti e sette mi si dimostraro: 'e' = and; tutti e sette 'all seven' (tutti concords with sette); mi si dimostraro is a dative/ventive plus reflexive construction meaning 'they showed themselves to me' — si is reflexive, dimostraro is an archaic/metrical passato remoto form of dimostrar (they displayed themselves). quanto son grandi / quanto son veloci are exclamative/comparative clauses introduced by quanto 'how' with son (present) + adjectives grandi, veloci. e come sono in distante riparo: come 'in what manner/how', sono present of essere, in distante riparo 'in a remote/distant refuge/abode' (riparo = shelter or retreat).
segments.paradiso-22-g143: L’aiuola 'the little plot/flower-border' is the subject; che introduces a relative clause 'that'; ci is the clitic for 'us' (indirect object or experiencer) and precedes the verb; fa is present indicative 'makes' (fa tanto feroci = 'makes us so fierce'), tanto intensifies feroci 'fierce'. The whole phrase describes cause/effect: the garden causes them to be fierce.
segments.paradiso-22-g144: volgendom’ io is the archaic/poetic inversion of volgendo + reflexive clitic mi (volgendomi), with the clitic attached and elided as -m’; it means 'as I turned myself' or 'turning myself'. io is optionally emphatic subject. con li etterni Gemelli: con = with; the article li (archaic plural) + etterni (old spelling of eterno) 'eternal'; Gemelli here names the twin stars/constellation (Gemini), so the phrase marks accompaniment or the point of orientation.
segments.paradiso-22-g145: tutta m’apparve: tutta 'all/entirely' modifies the subject that is understood from previous phrase; m’apparve = mi apparve (passato remoto) 'appeared to me'. da’ colli a le foci is a prepositional span 'from the hills to the mouths' (da' = da + il), with da indicating origin and a indicating extent/limit (to the river-mouths).
segments.paradiso-22-g146: poscia 'afterwards/then' introduces the next action. rivolsi is passato remoto of rivolgere (here transitive) 'I turned/directed' with the implied subject io; li occhi is the direct object 'the eyes' (li = the, occhi plural). a li occhi belli: a marks the direction 'to' (turned my eyes to), li occhi belli repeats/identifies the target 'those beautiful eyes'—belli functions as attributive adjective after the noun for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-22-g34: 'Ma' is the adversative 'but' introducing a contrast. 'perché' here means 'so that' (introducing a purpose/result clause) rather than 'why'; 'tu' is the stressed 2nd‑person subject (placed before the verb phrase for emphasis).
segments.paradiso-22-g35: 'aspettando' is the gerund of aspettare and means 'while waiting' / 'in waiting' — it gives the temporal circumstance in which what follows happens.
segments.paradiso-22-g36: The negative 'non' + 'tarde' (an archaic/poetic form of tardare) expresses 'do not delay'; syntactically it belongs to the subordinate clause introduced by 'perché'. 'a' is the preposition 'to', 'l’ alto fine' = 'the lofty end/goal' (l’ = definite article + alto 'lofty', fine 'end/goal').
segments.paradiso-22-g37: 'io' is the subject 'I' (fronted for emphasis). 'ti' is the indirect object 'to you'. 'farò' is the future indicative of fare ('I will make/give'); 'risposta' is a noun 'answer/response'. 'pur' here means 'even' or 'still' (emphatic: I will answer you even to...), and 'al pensier' = 'to the thought' (al = a + il).
segments.paradiso-22-g38: 'Da che' is a causal/concessive (literally 'since/inasmuch as'); the clause 'da che sì ti riguarde' gives the reason: 'since you so regard/concern yourself.' 'sì' = 'so', and 'riguarde' is an archaic form of riguardare meaning 'regard/concern' (here used in the sense 'care about / attend to').
segments.paradiso-22-g39: 'Quel monte' = 'that mountain.' The relative phrase 'a cui Cassino è ne la costa' = 'on whose slope Cassino is located' (ne = locative 'in/on' + la costa 'the slope'). 'Fu frequentato' is a passato remoto passive ('was frequented'), 'già' here means 'formerly/once', 'in su la cima' = 'on the summit', and 'da la gente ingannata e mal disposta' names the agent: 'by people deceived and ill‑disposed' (gente is treated as a collective and takes feminine participial agreement: ingannata, disposta).
segments.paradiso-22-g40: The clause opens with 'e quel son io' — a marked inversion: 'and that one am I' (i.e. I am that person). 'che sù vi portai prima' is a relative clause with passato remoto 'portai' ('I brought up there before'), 'vi' = 'there'. 'lo nome di colui che ’n terra addusse la verità' = 'the name of him who on earth brought/introduced the truth' ('addusse' = passato remoto of addurre, 'brought/led'). The final 'che tanto ci soblima' is a relative clause: 'which so uplifts/exalts us' (soblima = archaic present form meaning 'elevates' or 'exalts').
segments.paradiso-22-g41: 'E tanta grazia sopra me relusse' — 'and so much grace shone upon me.' 'relusse' is passato remoto of relucere (to shine), 'sopra me' indicates the grace shone 'upon me' (dative/locative effect).
segments.paradiso-22-g42: The result clause introduced by 'ch’' (che = 'that/so that') shows consequence: 'that I drew back the surrounding villas.' 'Ritrassi' is an archaic past form ('I drew back/withdrew' — poetically formed); 'le ville circunstanti' = 'the surrounding villages/estates.' 'da l’empio cólto che ’l mondo sedusse' names from whom: 'from the impious, cultivated one who led the world astray' (colto = 'cultivated/learned', sedusse = passato remoto of sedurre 'led astray/seduced').
segments.paradiso-22-g43: Word order is inverted for poetic effect: 'Questi altri fuochi tutti contemplanti uomini fuoro' = 'These other fires, all contemplative, were men.' 'Fuoro' is passato remoto of essere ('they were'). 'Contemplanti' is a present participle used adjectivally ('contemplative').
segments.paradiso-22-g44: 'accesi' is a past participle ('lit/kindled'), here participial predicate 'set alight by that warmth' — 'di quel caldo' = 'by/with that heat/warmth.' The relative clause 'che fa nascere i fiori e ’ frutti santi' uses present 'fa nascere' = 'causes to be born/produces' and modifies 'caldo' (that warmth produces holy flowers and fruits).
segments.paradiso-22-g45: 'Qui è Maccario' = 'Here is Maccario.' 'Qui' marks location/identification; 'è' is the copula 'is'; proper name follows in normal order.
segments.paradiso-22-g46: Same structure as the previous segment: 'qui è Romoaldo' = 'here is Romuald.' The simple copula identifies the person present.
segments.paradiso-22-g47: 'qui son li frati miei' = 'here are my brothers' (inversion: son = sono 'are'). The relative clause 'che dentro ai chiostri fermar li piedi e tennero il cor saldo' describes them: 'dentro ai chiostri' = 'within the cloisters'; 'fermar li piedi' ('stayed/stopped their feet') is a poetic/archaic expression meaning they halted their worldly steps, and 'tennero il cor saldo' ('they kept their heart steady') uses tennero (passato remoto) to describe their firm resolve. The two verb phrases coordinate the actions of those brothers.
segments.paradiso-22-g48: "E io a lui:" is an elliptical introductory phrase meaning "And I (said) to him:". 'E' links to the previous speech, 'io' is the subject, and the preposition 'a' marks 'lui' as the indirect object (to him).
segments.paradiso-22-g49: The relative clause begins with the noun phrase "L'affetto" (the affection) followed by the relative "che" introducing "dimostri": a present-tense 2nd-person verb describing what the addressee shows. "Meco" (with me) is an older, enclitic form meaning "with me," and "parlando" is a gerund (speaking) that qualifies how the affection is shown.
segments.paradiso-22-g50: This coordinated phrase "e la buona sembianza" (and the good/kind expression) is followed by another relative clause "ch'io veggio e noto" where "veggio" (Dante's form of 'vedere') and "noto" are first-person verbs meaning 'I see' and 'I note/observe'. The prepositional phrase "in tutti li ardor vostri" places those qualities 'in all your ardors' ("li" = the plural article).
segments.paradiso-22-g51: Main clause: "così m'ha dilatata mia fidanza." The adverb "così" ('thus/so') modifies the perfect construction "m'ha dilatata" (ha + past participle) where the clitic "m'" is the direct object 'me' — literally, 'has expanded me' meaning 'has enlarged my trust' ("mia fidanza"). The syntax is typical participial perfect with clitic object before the auxiliary.
segments.paradiso-22-g52: A simile introduced by "come" (as). The clause compares the enlargement of trust to a natural process: "'l sol fa la rosa quando aperta tanto divien quant' ell' ha di possanza." Grammatically, "fa" is present indicative 'makes', "quando aperta" is a temporal/absolute participial phrase 'when (it is) open', and "divien" is present 'becomes' followed by a comparative expression "quant' ell' ha di possanza" ('as much as it has of power').
segments.paradiso-22-g53: "Però ti priego" is a colloquial sequence meaning 'So/therefore I beg you' or 'I therefore entreat you.' Here "Però" introduces the request as a consequence; "ti" is the clitic object 'you', and "priego" is first-person present 'I ask/beg' (Dante's verb form).
segments.paradiso-22-g54: A brief address "e tu," ('and you,') that emphasizes the direct addressee; this tu is vocative/emphatic and anticipates the more formal address that follows.
segments.paradiso-22-g55: A single vocative noun "padre," addressed to the speaker's interlocutor; the comma marks direct address and politeness/formality (father = sir/paternal title).
segments.paradiso-22-g56: "m'accerta s'io posso prender tanta grazia" combines an imperative/requestal verb and a conditional content clause. "M'accerta" (literally 'make certain to me') is an imperative or hortative request meaning 'assure me' or 'ascertain for me'; "s'io posso prender" = 'whether I can take/receive' ("s'" = archaic 'se' = 'if/whether', "posso" = 'I can/may', "prender" = infinitive 'take/receive'), and "tanta grazia" is the object 'so great a grace'.
segments.paradiso-22-g57: Content clause introduced by "ch'" (= che, 'that'): "ch'io ti veggia con imagine scoverta." Here "veggia" is a subjunctive/present-form verb meaning 'may I see' (Dante's older/variant verb form), "con imagine scoverta" ('with image/appearance uncovered') qualifies how he would see the addressee. The sequence is a request for permission/assurance to see him unveiled.
segments.paradiso-22-g58: "Ond' elli:" is a narrative tag meaning 'whereupon he (replied):' — "ond'" (onde) indicates consequence or transition and the following pronoun indicates the speaker. This is a reduced formula introducing direct speech.
segments.paradiso-22-g59: "Frate," is a vocative address 'Brother,' used here to address the pilgrim in fraternal/monastic tone; it is an isolated interjection before the declarative sentence.
segments.paradiso-22-g60: Main assertion: "il tuo alto disio s'adempierà in su l'ultima spera." The subject phrase "il tuo alto disio" ('your lofty desire') is followed by the future verb "s'adempierà" (si adempierà) 'will be fulfilled.' The prepositional phrase "in su l'ultima spera" places the fulfillment 'up in the ultimate sphere' (Dante's cosmology: ultima spera = highest/outermost heaven).
segments.paradiso-22-g61: "ove s'adempion tutti li altri e 'l mio." "Ove" = 'where'; "s'adempion" (si adempiono) is a present plural verb 'are fulfilled' (note archaic orthography); "tutti li altri e 'l mio" lists what is fulfilled there: all the other desires and mine. The verb agrees with the plural subject implied ('they are fulfilled').
segments.paradiso-22-g62: "Ivi è perfetta," states existence/location: "Ivi" (there) + present copula "è" + predicate adjective "perfetta" — 'There it is perfect.' The construction asserts a quality present in that place/sphere.
segments.paradiso-22-g63: "matura e intera ciascuna disïanza;" lists three adjectives modifying the noun: "matura" (mature/ripe), "intera" (whole), applied to "ciascuna disïanza" = 'each distancing/longing' (Dante's coinage meaning degree of desire/separation). Word order places adjectives before the noun for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-22-g64: "in quella sola è ogne parte là ove sempr' era," is a locative clause: "in quella sola" ('in that single [sphere]') + "è ogne parte" ('is every part') + relative "là ove sempr' era" ('there where it always was'). The verb "è" links the existence/position of parts to their eternal place.
segments.paradiso-22-g65: "perché non è in loco e non s'impola;" gives the reason: "perché" = 'because', followed by two negated predicates. "Non è in loco" = 'it is not in place' (i.e., not spatially localized), and "non s'impola" (si impola) uses a reflexive/clitic passive-like construction whose literal root concerns 'poles' — Dante means it is not subject to polar/positional determination. The double negation emphasizes non-spatial character.
segments.paradiso-22-g66: "e nostra scala infino ad essa varca," coordinates the idea with "and our ladder reaches up to it." "Nostra scala" (our ladder) is the subject, "infino ad essa" (up to it) is a directional phrase, and "varca" (varcare) is present indicative 'crosses/steps across' used here transitively intransitive sense 'reaches/passes into' the sphere.
segments.paradiso-22-g67: "onde così dal viso ti s'invola." The relative/causal "onde" ('from which/whence') introduces the result: "così dal viso ti s'invola" — 'thus it flies away from your sight.' "Dal viso" ('from the face/sight') indicates source, "ti" is the clitic object 'from you' or 'from your sight,' and "s'invola" is a pronominal present 'flies away' (s- reflexive plus 'invola').
segments.paradiso-22-g68: Infin là sù is a compact adverbial phrase meaning 'at last/up there' (infin used adverbially). La is the feminine direct object pronoun/article (the ladder referred to from the prior context). Vide is the simple past (pretérito) 'saw' and is followed by the infinitive porger, so the construction means 'saw it extend/reach' (vide + infinitive = perceive an action). Superna parte is the object of porger: 'the heavenly/upper part.' Word order (adverbial → object → subject → verb → infinitive phrase) is a poetic inversion but preserves the meaning 'there up high the patriarch Jacob saw it reach the heavenly part.'
segments.paradiso-22-g69: Quando introduces a temporal clause 'when'. Li is the dative 'to him' (Jacob) and apparve is the perfect/simple past 'appeared.' D'angeli 'of/with angels' and carca is an adjective 'laden, full of' modifying the understood subject (the ladder); the clause means 'when it appeared to him, laden with angels.' Note the elision d’ for di before a vowel.
segments.paradiso-22-g70: Ma is the simple adversative conjunction 'but' introducing a contrast with the preceding idea.
segments.paradiso-22-g71: Per + infinitive (salirla) gives purpose or the action considered 'to climb it'/'in order to climb it'; salirla is the infinitive salire plus the clitic object la ('it'), meaning 'to climb it' (the ladder). The comma marks that this infinitival phrase frames the following statement.
segments.paradiso-22-g72: Mo is an archaic/poetic adverb meaning 'now' or 'at present'. Nessun is the indefinite 'no one'; diparte is present-tense reflexive/impersonal form of dipartire 'move away/leave' (here 'lifts/departs'); da terra i piedi is a fixed phrase 'his feet from the ground' (literally 'from earth the feet'). Word order places the temporal adverb first for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-22-g73: E connects the clause. La regola mia 'my rule' is the subject; rimasa is an archaic past participle/ adjective from rimanere ('left, remaining') placed before the copula è (a poetic inversion rimasa è = 'remains/has been left'); per danno de le carte is an idiomatic phrase 'because of the loss/damage of the papers/documents' (per here = 'because of/through'). The whole means the writer's rule survives only on paper because the documents are damaged/missing.
segments.paradiso-22-g74: Le mura 'the walls' is the subject; che solieno esser + noun is a relative clause using the imperfect of solere (solieno) plus the infinitive esser meaning 'that used to be ...'. Fatte sono is a passive construction with the past participle fatte placed before the copula sono ('have been made/are made'); spelonche is the predicate noun 'caves/caverns.' The line shows several poetic inversions (participial adjective before verb, archaic solieno).
segments.paradiso-22-g75: E connects clauses. Le cocolle (plural of cocolla, the monastic cowls/scapulars) sacca 'bags/pouches' son piene 'are full' di farina ria 'of wicked/bad flour' — an accusative of content. Son is the poetic form of sono. The noun-adjective sequence farina ria means 'spoiled/bad flour' in context.
segments.paradiso-22-g76: Ma introduces contrast. Grave usura 'serious/heavy usury' is the subject; tanto here intensifies ('so much/so grave'). Non si tolle uses the impersonal/reflexive si with the verb tollere ('is taken away/allowed/forgiven'); contra 'against' + 'l piacer di Dio' ('the pleasure/will of God') marks the thing opposed. The clause says such usury is not tolerated in opposition to God's will. Note archaic verb use and the impersonal si.
segments.paradiso-22-g77: Quanto introduces a comparative idea 'as much/as for' and quel frutto 'that fruit/result/— here 'profit' or the outcome' is the subject of the relative clause che fa il cor de' monaci sì folle: fa is present 'makes' and cor de' monaci is 'the hearts of the monks.' Sì folle modifies cor: 'so foolish.' The clause describes the harmful result that corrupts monks' hearts.
segments.paradiso-22-g78: Ché is a causal conjunction 'for/because'; quantunque here means 'however much' or 'although' and introduces a concessive clause. La Chiesa is the subject 'the Church' (institution) and guarda is present 'watches/guards/keeps an eye on.' The line concedes the Church's vigilance while preparing the following qualification.
segments.paradiso-22-g79: Tutto è de la gente 'everything belongs to the people' is a general statement (tutto = all/entirety). Che per Dio dimanda is a relative clause: che 'who' and per Dio dimanda 'asks/begs for God' (per = 'for/on behalf of'; dimanda = 'requests/begs'). The sense is that alms/benefits go to those who beg in God's name.
segments.paradiso-22-g80: Non di parenti né d’altro più brutto is a negative qualification: 'not to relatives nor to any other more shameful/vile [people].' Né is the negative conjunction 'nor', più modifies brutto ('more vile'), and the structure denies that the goods belong to kin or worse persons.
segments.paradiso-22-g120: An address in the second person: Tu (you) + se' is the contracted/poetic form of sei (you are). Sì modifies presso (so/very), and presso a + article + noun means 'near to' something; l’ultima salute is a poetic phrase meaning 'the final salvation'. Word order is standard for a predicate sentence (subject + verb + degree + prepositional phrase).
segments.paradiso-22-g121: cominciò is the third-person singular past (passato remoto) of cominciare — 'began' — introducing direct speech; Bëatrice is the proper name, the speaker.
segments.paradiso-22-g122: This is the start of Beatrice's quoted clause. che introduces a content clause ('that ...'). tu is the subject. dei is an archaic/poetic form here: it corresponds to 'devi' (you must) and governs the following infinitive aver; aver is the bare infinitive 'to have', and le luci tue 'your lights' is the direct object, with the adjectives chiare e acute following in normal adjective order.
segments.paradiso-22-g123: e però links the reasons: 'and therefore'. Però here has the logical sense 'therefore' (not adversative).
segments.paradiso-22-g124: prima che introduces a temporal clause 'before ...'. With prima che + verb, Dante uses a subjunctive-like or poetic construction; tu più t’inlei is addressed to 'you', più = 'further/any more', and t’inlei is reflexive (ti + inlei). Inlei is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning to involve or ensnare oneself (so 'before you further involve yourself').
segments.paradiso-22-g125: rimira is an imperative (or hortative) form of rimirare — 'look at/consider' — addressed to the second person; in giù = 'downwards' (giù after in emphasizes downward direction). The verb+prepositional phrase forms a simple command: 'look down.'
segments.paradiso-22-g126: e vedi continues the sequence of imperatives/commands ('and see'). quanto introduces the quantity or extent clause 'how much/as much as'; mondo here denotes 'the world/realm' (Dante's cosmic 'world'); sotto li piedi = 'under the feet' (li piedi = 'the feet') and già esser ti fei is a compact past construction: giÀ (already) + esser (infinitive 'to be') + ti (for you) + fei (archaic passato remoto of fare 'I made/caused'), yielding 'that I have already caused to be under your feet.'
segments.paradiso-22-g127: sì che introduces a result clause 'so that'. ’l tuo cor = 'your heart' (article + possessive + noun) with normal order.
segments.paradiso-22-g128: quantunque può is a concessive/limiting phrase: quantunque = 'however much' / 'to whatever extent' and può is third-person singular of potere 'can/is able'. Together here: 'as much as it can.'
segments.paradiso-22-g129: giocondo is an adjective meaning 'joyful' or 'glad' describing cor. s’appresenti is a reflexive verb (si + appresenti) in the present subjunctive/optative sense (third-person singular) 'present itself' or 'may present itself' in a mildly hortatory/result sense after sì che. a la turba trïunfante = 'to the triumphant throng' (a + definite article + noun + participle). The relative clause che lieta vien per questo etera tondo modifies 'turba': lieta vien = 'comes joyful'; per questo etera tondo = 'through this ethereal circle/round.' Note tondo is used poetically for the celestial round.
segments.paradiso-22-g130: Col is the contraction of con + il ('with the'), so Col viso = 'with the face' or idiomatically 'with my face/with my gaze'. ritornai is first-person singular passato remoto 'I returned' or 'I turned back'; per tutte quante le sette spere = 'through all seven spheres' (tutte quante emphasizes totality).
segments.paradiso-22-g131: e vidi = 'and I saw' (passato remoto vidi). questo globo = 'this globe/sphere' and tal = 'such' (an intensifier: 'so/so great' used poetically). The adjective tal follows the noun for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-22-g132: ch’io is the subordinating particle che + pronoun io (here used in an explanatory clause). sorrisi is the past tense of sorridere ('I smiled') — Dante uses the simple past. del suo vil sembiante = 'at/from its mean/paltry appearance' (del = di + il), with vil (paltry/base) qualifying sembiante (appearance).
segments.paradiso-22-g133: e quel consiglio = 'and that counsel'. per migliore approbo: approbo here is first-person singular present of approbare ('I approve'), so the speaker endorses that counsel as migliore ('better'). The phrase treats the speaker's judgement as present and explicit.
segments.paradiso-22-g134: che l’ha per meno is a relative clause: che = 'which'; l’ ha = 'has it' (l' = direct object pronoun 'it'); per meno = 'held as less' or 'valued less'. e chi ad altro pensa = 'and whoever thinks otherwise' (chi = whoever; ad altro pensa = thinks of/considers other); chiamar si puote veramente probo is an impersonal/expressive construction: chiamar (infinitive 'to call') + si puote ('one can' / 'may be called') + veramente probo = 'truly upright' — 'may truly be called upright.'
segments.paradiso-22-g135: Vidi is the past tense 'I saw'. la figlia di Latona = 'the daughter of Latona' (i.e., Diana). incensa is a past participle/adjective from incensare; in this context it means 'censed' or 'surrounded with incense' (literally 'incensed' with incense, a ritual image) rather than the modern sense 'angry'. sanza = an archaic form of senza, meaning 'without'. quell’ ombra = 'that shadow'. che mi fu cagione = a relative clause: 'which was the cause to me' (mi fu cagione = 'caused me' / 'was the reason that I ...'). per che già la credetti rara e densa = 'wherefore I already believed her rare and dense' (credetti is passato remoto 'I believed/took to be').
segments.paradiso-22-g136: L’aspetto = 'the appearance/aspect' (article + noun). del tuo nato = 'of your born one/your offspring' — nato here functions as a substantive ('the one born', i.e., your child). Word order is genitive construction: 'the aspect of your son/offspring.'
segments.paradiso-22-g137: Iperïone is the vocative/appositive name (Hyperion) identifying the 'tuo nato' — a proper name standing alone as an address or identification.
segments.paradiso-22-g138: quivi = 'there' (at that place). sostenni is first-person singular passato remoto of sostenere; here it can mean 'I sustained/held' or more loosely 'I kept before me / I beheld' — Dante often uses such verbs to say he 'held' an image in view.
segments.paradiso-22-g139: e vidi = 'and I saw.' com’ (short for come) = 'how/with what manner' and si move is the reflexive passive/mediopassive 'moves itself' or simply 'moves' (third-person singular). circa e vicino a lui = 'around and near him' (circa = around; vicino = near), and Maia e Dïone are the two proper names functioning as subjects that follow the motion verb in apposition: 'Maia and Dione move about and near him.'
segments.paradiso-22-g99: This clause is a main clause: definite article + adjective + noun (La dolce donna) form the subject; 'dietro a lor' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'behind them' (dietro + a + lor). 'Mi pinse' is a simple past (passato remoto) 3rd person singular 'pushed me' with 'mi' as direct object clitic. 'Con un sol cenno' is an instrumental phrase ('with a single gesture'); 'su per quella scala' indicates motion upward along that staircase (su + per + demonstrative + noun). Word order is typical: subject — location/adverbial phrases — verb.
segments.paradiso-22-g100: 'Sì' here is an intensifier ('so, thus'); 'sua virtù' (‘her power/virtue’) is the subject of the verb 'vinse', passato remoto 'overcame' (3rd sg.). 'La mia natura' is the direct object with the definite article before the possessive (la mia). The clause literally reads 'so her virtue my nature overcame' — a poetic inversion where 'sì' fronting emphasizes degree.
segments.paradiso-22-g101: This negative comparative clause begins with 'né mai' = 'nor ever'; 'qua giù' is a local adverbial ('here below'); 'dove si monta e cala naturalmente' is a relative/adverbial subordinate meaning 'where one naturally goes up and down' — 'si' is the impersonal/reflexive marker with 3rd-person verbs 'monta' and 'cala'. 'Naturalmente' qualifies the manner.
segments.paradiso-22-g102: 'Fu' is passato remoto of essere ('there was'); 'sì ratto moto' = 'so swift a movement' (sì + adjective + noun). 'Ch' introduces a result clause ('that'); 'agguagliar si potesse' is a passive/impersonal periphrasis with the reflexive 'si' plus imperfect subjunctive 'potesse' meaning 'could be matched' or 'could equal'. 'A la mia ala' is the indirect comparison target ('to my wing').
segments.paradiso-22-g103: The opening 'S'io torni mai' is an archaic contraction of 'Se io torni mai' meaning 'If ever I return'; 'torni' is present subjunctive used in conditional clauses after 'se' in older/poetic Italian. 'Mai' = 'ever'.
segments.paradiso-22-g104: A direct address vocative: 'lettore' = 'reader'. It stands alone and addresses the reader of the poem.
segments.paradiso-22-g105: 'A quel divoto trïunfo' is a prepositional phrase ('to that devout triumph'); 'per lo quale' introduces a relative clause 'for which' (or 'by which') explaining the triumph; 'io piango spesso le mie peccata e 'l petto mi percuoto' is the main statement of the relative clause: present indicative 'piango' and reflexive-like 'mi percuoto' (I beat my breast). Word order places the object 'le mie peccata' before the coordinating phrase.
segments.paradiso-22-g106: This clause ('tu non avresti ... nel foco il dito') uses the conditional perfect 'avresti ... messo' (you would have put) with negation 'non'. 'In tanto' = 'so much / to such an extent'; 'tratto' and 'messo' are past participles used with 'avresti' to mean 'you would have so drawn and put' — a figurative expression. 'Nel foco il dito' is literal 'the finger into the fire'.
segments.paradiso-22-g107: 'In quant' io vidi 'l segno' = 'as much as / when I saw the sign' — 'in quanto' here has a concessive/temporal sense. 'Che segue il Tauro' is a relative clause 'that follows Taurus (the constellation)'. 'E fui dentro da esso' uses passato remoto 'fui' ('I was') with 'dentro' ('inside') and the older preposition 'da' with 'esso' to indicate being within that sign; the clause explains the measure of the narrator's experience.
segments.paradiso-22-g108: An invocation: 'O' is the vocative particle (Oh); 'glorïose stelle' is a direct address in the vocative plural ('glorious stars'). The adjective follows the vocative particle as usual in poetry.
segments.paradiso-22-g109: 'O lume pregno di gran virtù' is a second vocative phrase: 'lume' (light) modified by 'pregno di gran virtù' meaning 'full/pregnant with great virtue' — 'pregno di' = 'full of'. Word order (noun + adjective phrase) is emphatic.
segments.paradiso-22-g110: 'Dal quale' literally 'by/from which' introduces a relative clause: 'io riconosco tutto' = 'I recognize/know all' — here 'dal quale' indicates the means by which the speaker recognizes. The verb 'riconosco' is present indicative, first person.
segments.paradiso-22-g111: 'Qual che si sia' is an idiomatic concessive formula meaning 'whatever (it) may be' — 'qual' + subjunctive 'sia' with reflexive/impersonal 'si' creates the indefinite sense. Word order is inverted for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-22-g112: Short noun phrase 'il mio ingegno' = 'my intellect/genius' with normal article + possessive + noun order. It is the object of 'riconosco' in the previous clause (elliptically separated by punctuation).
segments.paradiso-22-g113: Compound clause: 'con voi nasceva e s'ascondeva vosco' — 'nasceva' and 's'ascondeva' are both imperfects (3rd sg.) describing simultaneous action in the past: 'was born with you and hid himself with you.' 'Vosco' is an archaic/dialectal form meaning 'with you' (like 'vosco' = 'vossì'/'con voi'). 'Quelli ch'è padre d'ogne mortal vita' is a resumptive relative: 'that one who is father of every mortal life' referring to the person previously mentioned (the subject of the imperfect verbs).
segments.paradiso-22-g114: 'Quand' io senti' di prima l'aere tosco' = 'when I first felt the Tuscan air'. 'Quand'' is an elided form of 'quando'; 'senti'' is passato remoto1st sg. ('I felt/perceived'). 'Di prima' = 'at first' or 'of the first time'; 'aere tosco' = 'Tuscan air' (i.e., the Tuscan dialect/vocabulary — Dante often references 'aere tosco' to mean the language of Tuscany).
segments.paradiso-22-g115: A brief connective 'e poi' = 'and then / and afterwards' linking the temporal clauses; both words are adverbial/conjunctive.
segments.paradiso-22-g116: 'Quando mi fu grazia largita d'entrar ne l'alta rota che vi gira' = 'when grace was granted to me to enter into the high wheel that turns there.' 'Mi fu grazia largita' uses the impersonal/passive construction 'fu ... largita' (past tense 'was bestowed') with 'mi' as the indirect object; 'd'entrar' is an infinitive of purpose/compliment. 'Ne l'alta rota' = 'into the high wheel' and 'che vi gira' is a relative clause 'that turns there'.
segments.paradiso-22-g117: 'La vostra regïon mi fu sortita' is a passive/impersonal perfect: 'your region was allotted to me' — 'mi fu sortita' uses 'sortire' in the sense 'to be allotted/assigned' with 'mi' marking the person who receives the allotment. Word order places the possessed noun phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-22-g118: 'A voi divotamente ora sospira l'anima mia' is a dative address 'To you' (A voi) followed by the adverb 'divotamente' ('devoutly') and temporal adverb 'ora' ('now'). 'Sospira' is present indicative 3rd person singular 'sighs' and 'l'anima mia' is the subject placed after the verb (inversion for emphasis): literally 'To you devoutly now sighs my soul.'
segments.paradiso-22-g119: 'Per acquistar virtute al passo forte che a sé la tira' expresses purpose with 'per' = 'in order to' + infinitive 'acquistar' ('to acquire'). 'Virtute' is the object 'virtue/power'; 'al passo forte' = 'to/for the strong step' (al = a + il). The relative clause 'che a sé la tira' ('that to itself draws it') describes the step; 'a sé' is a reflexive/directional phrase and 'la' refers back to 'virtute' or 'anima' depending on sense (here 'la' refers to the soul/virtue drawn by the step).

sentences.paradiso-22-s21: The flesh of mortals is so weak,
sentences.paradiso-22-s22: Peter began without gold and without silver, and I with prayer and with fasting, and Francis humbly (gave up) his convent; and if you look at the beginning of each of them, and then consider where it has gone, you will see white turned to brown.
sentences.paradiso-22-s23: Truly the Jordan was turned back even more, and the sea fled, when God turned; marvelous to see that here is the aid.
sentences.paradiso-22-s24: Thus he said to me, and then withdrew to his company, and the company drew together; then, like a whirlwind, it all wrapped itself upward.
sentences.paradiso-22-s5: But now turn yourself toward others; for you will see many illustrious spirits, if, as I say, I restore their appearance.
sentences.paradiso-22-s6: As it pleased her, I turned my eyes back, and I saw a hundred little pearls that together adorned themselves more with mutual rays.
sentences.paradiso-22-s7: I stood like one who represses in himself the point of desire, and does not dare to ask, so fearful of excess; and the greater and most splendid of those little margaritas I made stand before (me), in order to please my desire.
sentences.paradiso-22-s8: Then I heard within her: 'If you were to see how the charity that burns between us (glows), your thoughts would be expressed.'
sentences.paradiso-22-s1: Overwhelmed with astonishment, I turned to my guide like a little child who always runs back to the place where he feels most secure; and she, like a mother who immediately aids her pale and yearning son with her voice, which is wont to set things right, said to me: "Do you not know that you are in heaven?"
sentences.paradiso-22-s2: And do you not know that heaven is entirely holy, and that what is done there comes from good zeal?
sentences.paradiso-22-s3: How the song would have transformed you — and I laughing (you can think it now) — after the cry has moved you so much; in which, if you had understood his entreaties, the vengeance that you will see before you die would already be known to you.
sentences.paradiso-22-s4: The sword up here does not cut in haste nor in slowness, but rather according to the perception of the man who awaits it, desiring or fearing.
sentences.paradiso-22-s33: Then the tempering of Jupiter appeared to me between the Father and the Son; then it became clear to me the change they make in their places; and all seven displayed themselves to me how great they are and how swift, and how they are in a distant refuge.
sentences.paradiso-22-s34: The little plot that makes us so fierce—turning I with the eternal Twins—all of it appeared to me from the hills to the river-mouths; afterwards I turned my eyes to those beautiful eyes.
sentences.paradiso-22-s9: But so that you, while waiting, do not delay toward the lofty goal, I will give you an answer even to the thought, since you so regard yourself.
sentences.paradiso-22-s10: That mountain on whose slope Cassino stands was once frequented on its summit by people deceived and ill‑disposed; and I am the one who first brought there the name of him who on earth brought the truth that so uplifts us; and so much grace shone upon me, that I drew back the surrounding villages from the impious, cultivated one who led the world astray.
sentences.paradiso-22-s11: These other fires — all contemplative — were men, set alight by that warmth which makes the holy flowers and fruits be born.
sentences.paradiso-22-s12: Here is Maccarius, here is Romuald, here are my brothers who inside the cloisters stayed their steps and kept their hearts steadfast.
sentences.paradiso-22-s13: And I to him: "The affection you show in speaking with me, and the kindly expression that I see and observe in all your ardors, has so enlarged my confidence, as the sun makes the rose — when opened — become as much as it has power to be."
sentences.paradiso-22-s14: Therefore I beg you — and you, father, assure me whether I may take so great a grace that I may see you with your image unveiled."
sentences.paradiso-22-s15: Whereupon he: "Brother, your lofty desire will be fulfilled in the highest sphere, where all the others and mine are fulfilled."
sentences.paradiso-22-s16: There every longing is perfect, ripe and whole; in that single sphere every part is where it always was, because it is not in place and is not polarized; and our ladder reaches up to it, whence thus it flies from your sight.
sentences.paradiso-22-s17: At last up there the patriarch Jacob saw it reach up to the heavenly part, when it appeared to him so laden with angels.
sentences.paradiso-22-s18: But, to climb it, now no one lifts his feet from the earth, and my rule remains only on paper through the loss of the documents.
sentences.paradiso-22-s19: The walls that used to be an abbey have been turned into caverns, and the cowl-bags are full of spoiled flour.
sentences.paradiso-22-s20: But serious usury is not so allowed contrary to the pleasure of God, as that fruit which makes the hearts of monks so foolish; for however much the Church watches, everything belongs to the people who beg for God, not to relatives nor to any other still more shameful [persons].
sentences.paradiso-22-s29: "You are so near to the final salvation," began Beatrice, "that you must have your lights clear and keen; and therefore, before you further involve yourself, look down and see how much of the world I have already made to be beneath your feet; so that your heart, as much as it can, joyful, present itself to the triumphant throng that comes glad through this ethereal round."
sentences.paradiso-22-s30: With my face I turned through all seven spheres, and I saw this globe so that I smiled at its mean appearance; and I approve that counsel as better which holds it as less; and whoever thinks otherwise can truly be called upright.
sentences.paradiso-22-s31: I saw the daughter of Latona censed without that shadow that caused me, wherefore I had already taken her to be rare and dense.
sentences.paradiso-22-s32: I sustained (beheld) the aspect of your child, Hyperion, there, and I saw how Maia and Dione move about and near him.
sentences.paradiso-22-s25: The sweet lady pushed me from behind among them with a single gesture up that staircase; so much did her power overcome my nature; nor, here below, where one goes up and down naturally, was there ever so swift a movement that could be matched to my wing.
sentences.paradiso-22-s26: If ever I return, reader, to that devout triumph for which I often weep my sins and strike my breast, you would not have so far drawn and put your finger into the fire as I saw the sign that follows Taurus and was inside it.
sentences.paradiso-22-s27: O glorious stars, O light pregnant with great virtue, by which I recognize everything — whatever my intellect may be — he who is father of every mortal life was born with you and was hidden with you when I first felt the Tuscan air; and later, when grace was granted me to enter into the high wheel that turns there, your region was allotted to me.
sentences.paradiso-22-s28: To you my soul now sighs devoutly, in order to acquire virtue for the strong step that draws it to itself.
